sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 65-year-old female with a recent diagnosis of stage three non-small cell lung cancer, who is currently undergoing neo-adjuvant chemotherapy.
She reports shortness of breath, which began over the weekend, and has reduced her ability to walk her dog from a mile-long walk to a short walk down the street.
She also reports a dry cough, no fever, and pain upon swallowing that is located in her chest, but not in her throat.
She has been taking Ensure and other liquids, but has reduced her intake due to pain.
She has been on a combination of cisplatin and etoposide chemotherapy, and has experienced some nausea and fatigue, which was managed with Zofran.
Past medical history: The patient has a diagnosis of stage three non-small cell lung cancer.
Review of systems: The patient reports shortness of breath, dry cough, pain upon swallowing, and some fatigue.
Current medications: I prescribed Zofran [dose not specified] [frequency not specified] for chemotherapy-induced nausea.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation are normal.
Physical exam: The patient has a normal pulse, regular heart rate and rhythm, and no murmurs.
The lung exam reveals bilateral crackles, which may indicate fluid in the lungs due to the lung cancer diagnosis.
The skin exam shows erythema on the anterior aspect of the chest on the left side, which may be a radiation burn.
There is no lower extremity edema in the patient's legs.
Test Results: The patient had a chest X-ray that showed mild radiation pneumonitis, which is inflammation of the lungs due to radiation.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has stage three non-small cell lung cancer, radiation pneumonitis, and odynophagia (painful swallowing).
Problem list: Non-small cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; odynophagia.
Plan:
I prescribed prednisone 40mg one tablet a day for five days to reduce inflammation in the lungs and help the patient breathe better.
I also prescribed lidocaine spray to be used four times a day to alleviate painful swallowing.
Follow-up: The patient will return in two weeks to follow up on her treatment and to assess the effectiveness of the medications.
